evidence_quotes.quote: I would appreciate the DFA really tackling free trials that automatically turn into paid subscriptions. The consumer should have to opt in to the paid version after the free trial instead of having to opt out of it.
Having to show your ID to access content in the internet like it's been implemented in the UK is not acceptable.
Many advertisers use fake menus that act like real notifications from the user's phone to get the user to click the advertisement and be redirected to their site.

software including games, but also software used for editing or the creation of music or so many other things, that were sold to the customer should remain in a playable / useable state after the company and the developers have shut it down.
Payment processors should not have the ability to interfere with the sale of products in any way.
The example I'm obviously referring to is the outright censorship of the game stores Steam and itch.io that was enforced by Visa and Mastercard, threatening to pull out.

paragraphs.text: Hello! I like the direction this initiative is going, and to add to it, I'd like to have a few words on it. ||| So first, I'll talk about the points that have already been made in the discussion of the Digital Fairness Act (referred to as DFA from now on): || 1.) I would appreciate the DFA really tackling free trials that automatically turn into paid subscriptions. The consumer should have to opt in to the paid version after the free trial instead of having to opt out of it. || 2.) The protection of minors online is a good idea. Protecting them more in the case of advertisements and usage of data is great! However, the way minors are starting to be "protected" in other countries in not ok. Having to show your ID to access content in the internet like it's been implemented in the UK is not acceptable. This is a road leading directly to espionage on every consumer, incredible damage to privacy and the regulation does not actually fullfill its goal, since most of the barriers can be dodged by using a VPN. So, I do not want the DFA following the path the UK has taken. || 3.) Combating manipulative UI is great. Many advertisers use fake menus that act like real notifications from the user's phone to get the user to click the advertisement and be redirected to their site. Also, advertisers use design mimicking the UI elements the user is looking for and trick them into clicking. On download pages for example, advertisers use fake download buttons in their advertisements to bait the user into clicking. ||| Now to points I'd like to see added to the DFA: || 1.) I'm joining in with many other people giving feedback on this site saying that software including games, but also software used for editing or the creation of music or so many other things, that were sold to the customer should remain in a playable / useable state after the company and the developers have shut it down. For singleplayer games and e. g. editing software, this is relatively simple: Remove DRM-checks if present and otherwise just leave it for the consumer to play and use. For multiplayer games (and other software that is used with multiple, connected users), the solution could be the ability of the consumer to self-host the software at their own risk. Software made in the future should always have to be created with an end-of-life plan in mind. || 2.) Payment processors should not have the ability to interfere with the sale of products in any way. They should provide a way to pay for any legal product and not act like a "morale barrier" that "protects" all consumers from (in their opinion) "unsuitable products". The example I'm obviously referring to is the outright censorship of the game stores Steam and itch.io that was enforced by Visa and Mastercard, threatening to pull out. These payment processors should always have to process the payment, unless it is against the law. It's unacceptable for them have the power to decide what the consumer can buy. ||| Thank you for the consideration. I appreciate the DFA and with a few additions, changes and notes, I believe that it can strengthen consumer rights immensly, not just in the EU, but around the whole world.
